events.title: MH17 Departs Amsterdam Schiphol
events.detail: Malaysia Airlines Flight 17, a Boeing 777-200ER (registration 9M-MRD), departs Amsterdam Schiphol Airport at 10:15 UTC bound for Kuala Lumpur. The flight carries 283 passengers and 15 crew — 298 people from 17 nationalities. All pre-flight checks were normal and the aircraft was airworthy.

events.title: MH17 Enters Eastern Ukrainian Airspace at FL330
events.detail: MH17 enters the conflict zone over eastern Ukraine at cruising altitude of 33,000 feet (FL330). Ukrainian airspace below FL320 had been closed to civilian traffic due to the ongoing armed conflict, but MH17's altitude was above that restriction. The JIT later found that the risk assessment for flights above FL320 was inadequate.

sources.name: Dutch Safety Board Final Report, Oct 2015

events.title: Buk Missile Fired from Pervomaysk Field
events.detail: At approximately 13:20 UTC, a 9M38 series Buk surface-to-air missile is launched from a field near Pervomaysk, south of Snizhne, in separatist-held eastern Ukraine. The missile detonates to the left and above the cockpit with a 9N314M warhead, releasing approximately 800 high-energy fragments into the aircraft structure.

sources.name: Dutch Safety Board Final Report, Oct 2015

events.title: MH17 Disintegrates at 32,998 Feet
events.detail: MH17 breaks apart within seconds of the missile detonation at 32,998 feet above sea level. The cockpit section separates first, followed by the forward fuselage. Wreckage and 298 victims scatter across approximately 50 square kilometers of farmland in eastern Ukraine near the village of Hrabove, Donetsk Oblast. Last contact with air traffic control is lost at 13:20 UTC.

sources.name: Dutch Safety Board Final Report, Oct 2015

events.title: Girkin Posts Claim of 'AN-26' Shootdown, Then Deletes It
events.detail: Within hours of the crash, Igor Girkin (Strelkov), the self-styled DNR 'Defense Minister', posts on VKontakte (VK): 'In the vicinity of Torez, we just downed a plane, an AN-26. It is lying somewhere in the Progress Mine. We have issued warnings not to fly in our airspace.' When it becomes clear the aircraft was a civilian Boeing 777, the post is deleted. The post is preserved by internet archives and becomes key evidence.

events.title: Buk TELAR Returns to Russia with One Missile Missing
events.detail: The Buk TELAR (transporter-erector-launcher-and-radar) is photographed in Luhansk on the return journey later on July 17, now visibly missing one missile from its original four. The Buk travels east via Luhansk and crosses back into Russia. Photos of both the outbound journey (four missiles) and inbound return (three missiles) become foundational OSINT evidence for JIT.

events.title: UN Security Council Adopts Resolution 2166
events.detail: The UN Security Council unanimously adopts Resolution 2166, calling for a full, thorough and independent international investigation; demanding safe, full and unrestricted access to the crash site for investigators; and calling on all parties to cooperate. Australia sponsors the resolution. Russia — which normally would veto — votes in favor after text is negotiated (replacing 'shooting down' with 'downing').

events.title: Black Boxes Recovered and Handed to Malaysian Authorities
events.detail: Separatist forces hand over the Flight Data Recorder (FDR) and Cockpit Voice Recorder (CVR) — the 'black boxes' — to Malaysian aviation officials after international pressure. The black boxes, recovered from the crash site, show no evidence of tampering. They are analyzed in the United Kingdom by the Air Accidents Investigation Branch and later in the Netherlands.

events.title: First Repatriation Flights Land at Eindhoven
events.detail: The first aircraft carrying the remains of MH17 victims arrives at Eindhoven Air Base in the Netherlands. Dutch King Willem-Alexander, Queen Máxima, and Prime Minister Mark Rutte attend the solemn ceremony. The repatriation process takes many months as victim identification — complicated by the wide debris field — proceeds through DNA and dental analysis.

events.title: OSCE Monitors Granted Intermittent Crash-Site Access
events.detail: OSCE Special Monitoring Mission observers are granted intermittent access to the crash site in eastern Ukraine, where fighting between Ukrainian forces and Russian-backed separatists continues. Observers document bodies left unrecovered and evidence scattered across farmland. The chaotic conditions and ongoing fighting severely hamper early evidence collection and cause international outrage.

events.title: Joint Investigation Team (JIT) Established
events.detail: The Netherlands, Australia, Belgium, Malaysia, and Ukraine formally establish the Joint Investigation Team (JIT) to conduct the criminal investigation into the crash. The Netherlands — as the state with the most victims — leads the JIT. The Dutch National Prosecutor's Office, led by Fred Westerbeke (and later Dedy Woei-A-Sioe), heads the prosecution. Russia is notably absent despite invitations to join.

events.title: DSB Preliminary Report: External Cause Confirmed
events.detail: The Dutch Safety Board (DSB) releases its preliminary safety report, confirming that MH17 broke apart in flight as a result of structural damage caused by 'a large number of high-energy objects that penetrated the aircraft from outside.' The report stops short of identifying the weapon but eliminates mechanical failure and pilot error, pointing decisively to an external projectile.

events.title: Bellingcat Publishes Buk OSINT Investigation
events.detail: Open-source investigative outlet Bellingcat publishes 'Origin of the Separatists' Buk', using geolocated social media photos to trace the specific Buk TELAR (identified by unique markings) from Russia into eastern Ukraine on July 17 and back. Bellingcat's work — combining satellite imagery, social media posts, and photo analysis — eventually identifies the vehicle as belonging to the Russian military's 53rd Anti-Aircraft Missile Brigade in Kursk.

events.title: Russia Presents Multiple Contradictory Alternative Theories
events.detail: Russian authorities and state media advance several mutually contradictory alternative theories for MH17: (1) a Ukrainian Su-25 fighter shot down the plane; (2) a Ukrainian Buk fired the missile; (3) the missile was fired from Ukrainian army-controlled territory. Russian Ministry of Defence presents radar data and press conferences. JIT and independent analysts systematically debunk each theory.

events.title: Russia Vetoes UN International Tribunal for MH17
events.detail: Russia vetoes a UN Security Council resolution (sponsored by Malaysia, Australia, the Netherlands, Belgium, and Ukraine) that would have established an international criminal tribunal to prosecute those responsible for shooting down MH17. Russia casts the only vote against; China abstains. The veto forces the investigation to proceed through national Dutch courts. The Netherlands and Australia formally condemn Russia's veto.

events.title: DSB Final Safety Report: Buk 9N314M Warhead Confirmed
events.detail: The Dutch Safety Board publishes its comprehensive 279-page final safety report. The report confirms that MH17 was struck by a 9N314M-type warhead carried by a 9M38-series Buk surface-to-air missile, detonating above and to the left of the cockpit. The report also criticizes Ukrainian authorities for not closing higher altitudes and notes inadequate risk assessment by airlines and aviation authorities.

events.title: Victim Identification Process Spans Two Years
events.detail: The Dutch Forensic Institute (NFI) leads a painstaking identification process using DNA analysis, dental records, and fingerprints. The wide scatter of remains across the conflict zone, ongoing fighting, and looting at the crash site severely complicate efforts. The last victim was identified in 2019 — five years after the crash. The identification effort involves forensic teams from all victim nations.

events.title: JIT First Public Briefing: Buk Convoy Photos and Intercepts Released
events.detail: The Joint Investigation Team holds its first major public briefing, presenting photographs of the Buk convoy traveling through Donetsk on July 17, 2014, telephone intercepts between separatist commanders and Russian handlers discussing the 'Buk' and MH17 aftermath, and witness testimony from 800+ people interviewed. JIT confirms MH17 was shot down by a 9M38 series Buk missile and calls on the public to provide further information.

events.title: Bellingcat Identifies Buk as 53rd Brigade's Unit 332
events.detail: Bellingcat researchers publish detailed analysis identifying the specific Buk TELAR as belonging to the 53rd Anti-Aircraft Missile Brigade of the Russian Ground Forces, based in Kursk, Russia. The unit number (332) is identified through side-by-side comparison of photos taken during the July 17 convoy with photos from known 53rd Brigade exercises. This finding is later corroborated and confirmed by the JIT.

events.title: JIT Confirms Buk from Russia's 53rd Anti-Aircraft Brigade, Kursk
events.detail: The JIT holds a major press conference confirming that the Buk TELAR that shot down MH17 belonged to the 53rd Anti-Aircraft Missile Brigade of the Russian Armed Forces, based in Kursk. Detective Chief Superintendent Wilbert Paulissen states: 'The BUK-TELAR which was used came from the 53rd Brigade which is part of the military of the Russian Federation.' Russia denies the finding and rejects JIT conclusions.

events.title: Netherlands and Australia Formally Hold Russia Responsible
events.detail: Following the JIT's May 24 briefing, the governments of the Netherlands and Australia jointly notify Russia that they hold it responsible under international law for the deployment of the Buk missile that shot down MH17. The declaration initiates a state responsibility process and creates the basis for potential civil litigation and inter-state proceedings at international bodies including the ECHR and ICAO.

sources.name: Australian Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade

events.title: JIT Names Four Suspects: Girkin, Dubinsky, Pulatov, Kharchenko
events.detail: The JIT announces that four individuals will face prosecution for the murder of 298 people: Igor Girkin (Russian, former FSB colonel and DNR 'defense minister'), Sergey Dubinsky (Russian, GRU intelligence officer), Oleg Pulatov (Russian, deputy commander), and Leonid Kharchenko (Ukrainian, separatist commander). All are charged with causing the crash and murdering the 298 on board. An international arrest warrant is issued for all four.

events.title: Russia Provides Limited Cooperation, Blocks Key Evidence
events.detail: Throughout the investigation Russia's level of cooperation with the JIT remains minimal. Russia refuses to extradite suspects (its constitution prohibits extradition of Russian citizens), fails to respond to formal requests for military records identifying the 53rd Brigade Buk owners, and submits radar data to JIT that analysts determine is manipulated. The JIT publicly criticizes Russia's lack of substantive cooperation multiple times.

events.title: Murder Trial Opens at Schiphol Judicial Complex
events.detail: The criminal trial of the four MH17 suspects opens at the Schiphol Judicial Complex (Justitieel Complex Schiphol), a specially secured high-capacity courtroom near Amsterdam airport. Three defendants — Girkin, Dubinsky, and Kharchenko — are tried in absentia. Oleg Pulatov is the only suspect who appoints a legal defense team to represent him at trial.

events.title: JIT Presents Comprehensive Evidence Including OSINT and Intercepts
events.detail: Over two years of proceedings, the JIT presents its full evidence package to the Dutch District Court: geolocated photographs of the Buk convoy, 150,000 intercepted phone and radio communications, satellite imagery, witness testimony from hundreds of witnesses, forensic analysis of Buk missile fragments recovered from the crash site and victims' bodies, and corroborating OSINT analysis.

events.title: Prosecution Requests Life Imprisonment for All Four Defendants
events.detail: The Dutch prosecution concludes its case and requests life imprisonment for all four defendants, arguing that they knowingly caused the death of 298 civilians. Prosecutors argue that Girkin and Dubinsky were instrumental in requesting and directing the Buk system from Russia, and that all four bear criminal responsibility for the murder. Pulatov's defense team argues he was not in the vicinity of the launch.

events.title: Girkin, Dubinsky, and Kharchenko Convicted of Murder; Life Sentences
events.detail: The Netherlands District Court delivers its verdict: Igor Girkin, Sergey Dubinsky, and Leonid Kharchenko are convicted of murder and sentenced to life imprisonment in absentia. The court finds that all three caused the deaths of 298 people by directing or assisting the deployment of a Russian military Buk missile system to shoot down MH17. All three remain in Russia or occupied Ukraine.

events.title: Pulatov Acquitted: Insufficient Evidence of Direct Criminal Intent
events.detail: Oleg Pulatov, the only MH17 suspect with legal representation at trial, is acquitted. The court finds insufficient evidence that Pulatov was in the vicinity of the Buk launch site at the time of the launch, and there were no indications he could have intervened or changed the outcome. His acquittal is widely reported as the only partial legal victory for the Russian side.

events.title: Russia Rejects Conviction as 'Politically Motivated'
events.detail: Russia's Foreign Ministry and Kremlin reject the Dutch court's verdict, calling it 'politically motivated' and a 'mockery of justice.' Russia reiterates its denial of any involvement in the MH17 shootdown and refuses to extradite the convicted individuals. Russian state media portrays the verdict as an example of Western anti-Russian bias. The Russian constitution prohibits extradition of Russian citizens.

events.title: Girkin Arrested in Russia on Domestic Extremism Charges
events.detail: Igor Girkin, one of the three MH17 convictees, is arrested in Russia in July 2023 — not for MH17, but for inciting extremism through social media posts criticizing President Putin's handling of the Ukraine war. He had been posting sharp criticism of the Russian military command from his Telegram channel. His arrest is widely seen as a consequence of his political commentary rather than his MH17 conviction.

events.title: Netherlands and Ukraine Pursue Inter-State Case Against Russia at ECHR
events.detail: The Netherlands files an inter-state application at the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) against Russia over MH17. The case seeks to establish Russia's state responsibility under the European Convention on Human Rights. Following Russia's expulsion from the Council of Europe in March 2022, the legal proceedings continue under specific mechanisms. Victims' families also file individual ECHR applications.

events.title: Netherlands Files ICAO Complaint Against Russia
events.detail: The Netherlands files a complaint with the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) against Russia under the Convention on International Civil Aviation, arguing Russia violated international aviation law by deploying the Buk missile system that destroyed MH17 in civilian airspace. The ICAO arbitration process runs parallel to other proceedings and could result in binding findings on aviation safety obligations.

events.title: National MH17 Memorial Unveiled at Vijfhuizen
events.detail: The Nationaal Monument MH17 at Vijfhuizen, near Amsterdam, serves as the permanent memorial to the 298 victims. Sunflower installations and individual memorial elements represent the victims. Annual commemoration services are held on July 17 each year. The memorial is a gathering point for families from the Netherlands, Australia, Malaysia, Indonesia, and other victim nations.

events.title: JIT Shares Final Findings with Next of Kin
events.detail: On February 8, 2023, the Joint Investigation Team officially shares its final consolidated findings with the next of kin of MH17 victims. The JIT publishes a comprehensive summary of the criminal investigation covering all evidence gathered over nearly nine years. The JIT formally concludes its criminal investigation, though prosecution of the three convicted individuals remains pending their capture.
